html how to get jquery using script tag

To use jQuery in your HTML file, you need to include a script tag in your HTML file that references the jQuery library. Here's how you can do it:

1. First, go to the jQuery website and download the latest version of the jQuery library. You can also use a CDN to reference the library from a remote server.

2. Once you have downloaded or referenced the jQuery library, add a script tag in your HTML file within the head section to include the library. You can either reference the downloaded jQuery file or use the link to the jQuery CDN. Here's an example of how to reference the jQuery library from the jQuery CDN:

```
<head>
  <script src="https://code.jquery.com/jquery-3.6.0.min.js"></script>
</head>
```

3. Once you have included the jQuery library in your HTML file, you can then start using its functions in your script tags. Here's an example of how to use jQuery's document ready function to execute some code once the page has loaded:

```
<script>
  $(document).ready(function() {
    // your code here
  });
</script>
```

This will make sure that your code only executes once the document is fully loaded and rendered.

Note that in the example above, we used the `$` symbol to access jQuery's functions. The `$` symbol is a shorthand for `jQuery` and allows you to access all of jQuery's functions.
does the jquery script go on head or body

The jQuery script should go in the head section of your HTML document.

In general, it's best practice to include all your script tags, including jQuery, at the bottom of the body element before the closing `</body>` tag for faster page loading. However, to avoid potential issues with jQuery not being loaded before other scripts, it's recommended to include jQuery in the head section first.

Here's an example of how to include jQuery in the head section of your HTML document:

```
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <script src="https://code.jquery.com/jquery-3.6.0.min.js"></script>
  <title>My Website</title>
</head>
<body>
  <!-- Your HTML content here -->
  <script>
    // Your jQuery code here
  </script>
</body>
</html>
```
Note that this is just an example and you can modify it according to your needs.
how to setup php on comptuer so php works with index.html form

To set up PHP on your computer, you need to install a web server software (Apache, Nginx, IIS, etc.) and PHP itself. Here are some general steps to help you set up PHP on your computer:

1. Download and install a web server software. If you're using Windows, you can download and install XAMPP, which includes Apache, MySQL, PHP, and other useful tools.

2. Install PHP. XAMPP comes with PHP already installed, but if you're using another web server software, you might need to install PHP separately. You can download the latest version of PHP from the official PHP website.

3. Configure your web server to support PHP. If you're using XAMPP, PHP is already configured by default. If you're using another web server software, you might need to configure it manually. Refer to the documentation of your web server software for instructions on how to do this.

4. Create a simple PHP file (e.g., `test.php`) with some PHP code in it. Here's an example of a PHP file that displays "Hello, World!" on the screen:

```
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
	<title>My Website</title>
</head>
<body>
	<?php echo "Hello, World!"; ?>
</body>
</html>
```

Place this file in your web server's document root directory (usually `/var/www/html` for Linux or `C:\xampp\htdocs` for Windows if you're using XAMPP).

5. Finally, access the PHP file through a browser by entering `http://localhost/test.php` in the address bar. If everything is set up correctly, you should see "Hello, World!" displayed on the screen.

Note that to use PHP in an HTML file, the file needs to have a `.php` extension, not `.html`. If you want to include PHP code in an HTML file, you can rename the HTML file to `.php` and use the PHP code as shown in the example above.

how to get every device on same wifi if  i use arp -a and it shows know one but me

If `arp -a` does not show any other devices connected to your Wi-Fi network, it's possible that either there are no other devices connected to your network, or your network is configured in a way that prevents other devices from being detected through ARP.

Here are a few possible reasons why `arp -a` might not show any other devices on your Wi-Fi network:

1. No other devices are connected to your Wi-Fi network. This is unlikely, but it's possible that no other devices are currently connected to your network.

2. Devices on your network are using a different IP address range. If the devices on your network are using a different IP address range than your computer, `arp -a` will not detect them. You can try scanning your network using an IP scanner like Angry IP Scanner or Fing to see if any other devices show up.

3. Devices on your network are configured to not respond to ARP requests. Some devices, particularly mobile devices like smartphones and tablets, are configured to not respond to ARP requests as a security measure. In this case, `arp -a` would not be able to detect them.

4. Your router is configured to isolate devices. Some routers have a feature called "client isolation" that isolates devices on the network from each other for security reasons. If this feature is enabled, `arp -a` will not be able to detect other devices on the network.

Overall, `arp -a` is not always the most reliable way to detect devices on your network. To get a more comprehensive list of devices connected to your network, you can try using an IP scanner or a network monitoring tool like Wireshark.
